speaker: interviewer
text: You ready? OK. We were in Chatham. You had just arrived in Chatham. Talking about the people that you met there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were at Chatham and I was walking about, for about two months, with a broomstick in my hand. Anybody asked me, what are you doing? I would say, room orderly, sweeping up the front. I was walking about like this for about three months, before we-- we moved to an RAF place to do guard duty. It was Thorney Island, where the Lancaster bombers was. And after that, we got moved to near Ramsgate-- [? place ?] a seaside town near Ramsgate. And from there, I went back to Chatham. They've got dungeons-- they've got forts that were built in the Napoleon wars, because they thought Napoleon was going to come up the River Medway. And they had these forts and I was in one of these forts. And then one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they got a new GO-- a general officer commanding. It was a little unknown command. It was known as the southeast-- southeast-- southeast command, because the commands were split up. England was split up into sections. Eastern Command, Northern Command, Southern Command. And each command had a different-- if you was in Eastern Command, you was at the southern part of England, you went overseas. If you was North, then you was up north for training. And it was all worked out. And then, they got this new officer commanding, and he must have looked through what he's got there. He saw he's got over 3,000 troops there doing nothing, doing exactly nothing. So he started to break the whole thing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to do what they called-- like initiative tests. All sorts of-- they'd give you a bicycle pump, take it to pieces, and they'd ask you to screw it together. And they'd time it. The idea was to see if you was mechanically minded.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you knew how to put a bicycle pump together, or if you put an electric light holder together. They'd dismantle it and see if you-- which way your mind worked. If you was mechanically minded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they would ask you to take-- go from point A to point B, from point B to point C, and from point C-- to go around quickly. So I said, what's the object of that? They said, if you do it quickly, you're going to the artillery so you can take the shells from point A to point B. You can do it quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they would give you forms to fill out. They'd give you a lot of puzzles, what they give children. They'd give you three-- no-- three pictures on top and one blank, and on the bottoms is four pictures. On one of the pictures, the bottom fitted the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as soon as I saw it, I-- I saw what was in it. The trick was not to look at the pictures at the top, you pick-- looked at the picture at the bottom. Which is the odd one at the bottom, fitted the top. So one time, that-- it came out that they were going to send me off to the military police. Because I was good at paperwork. But they said, the military police is a voluntary organization. You can't-- you don't get called up with the military police. You volunteer for it. And if you don't like it, you can go out. You can always get out and you'll get sent somewhere else. So I went to the-- I got posted in military police, it must have been early '42. Just after '42. That was in-- near a little shop place called Mytchett-- Mytchett Barracks. And that was condemned in the first World War, but they're still using it. And then I met the chap there, someone I knew from London, so I said, what's the sense of this? He says, it's the cushiest job in the army. He says, nobody interferes with you, you do what you like. [LAUGHS] Who's going to interfere with a policemen? He says-- but they had three branches. One was what they call the Provo, for the discipline. One was traffic-- what they call traffic control. It was like military AA.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- signs-- put road signs up everywhere, if someone broke down, you helped them out, or whatever. One was called VPs, vital points. Usually guard duties, on vital points. What they called-- the docks, the railways, electricity, power stations, they shouldn't be sabotaged and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, go for the traffic. He says, don't go for the Provo, you go for the traffic. You get the motorcycle, any trouble, you're on your own. You've got your motorcycle and you can go. So I transferred, and I went to-- it's northern, a place called Cornbury Park. That belonged to Sullly Joe. But we was in the field-- so we was in a tent. We was there, a couple of years, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved on-- then we went to a little place called Much Hadham in the Bishop Stortford. And there we prepared for the invasion of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the invasion came about, where were you, prior?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was in Guildford. In Guildford, it was-- from Guildford, a few months before the invasion, we were shifted to the London Glider Club. Near Luton, is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Dunstable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dunstable. London Glider Club. Dunstable. On Dunstable Downs. And we knew-- we knew it was France because we got phrase books, how to say it in French. So we knew it was France. And then they-- they said, all the vehicles-- not the motorcycles, you couldn't take the motorcycles-- but all the lorries, had to be, what they called, waterproofed and fireproofed. They put a clay around the plugs, and the-- and the electrical systems, and then they had another clay for waterproofing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when you go off the landing barge, you go through the-- maybe, a few yards of water. So it shouldn't affect the engine, and the exhausts were shifted around. Instead of being under the vehicle, they were shifted around and they went up the sides of the vehicle, out just past the roof. So the exhaust was actually-- and if you look at today, at the-- at the big lorries, they've got the exhaust going up. Not the long side, but they go up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- what-- and one was to seal the petrol tanks. But once the petrol tanks were sealed, we knew you couldn't-- there's only one place you can go. Down to the docks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was on a Sunday, and we worked it out then the D-Day must be on the Tuesday. Because by the time you get down to the docks, and you can only go one way, you can't fill up anymore. But something happened in the War Office. They actually started the invasion, and something happened, it was canceled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put it back 24 hours. And I think it was June 6-- I think it was on a Wednesday. We went down from Millwall Docks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? You were based in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were from Dunstable. We went down to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went straight down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight, we drove straight down to the docks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your port of-- point of embarkation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they called it Millwall Docks. East End of Dock Road. From there, we went on the boat-- the liberty boats. Then the rumors start going around-- they're only welded together. They fall to pieces in the sea. [LAUGHS] And then when we set sail, Montgomery said, an order of the day, he says, you know, you've been training for this, and you've been waiting for this. And once you set sail, I can guarantee your safety for three miles out to sea. After that, you're in the hands of Almighty God. God bless you all and good luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went out. We left the Millwall Docks. And something went wrong-- there was a storm or something, so we went twice around the Isle of Wight at night, and then we made a dash across to the channel. To France-- Arromanches.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we got there, the gales blew up. We couldn't land. So we was on the boats for about four days. And then eventually, we-- we had to go off the liberty boat, on a rope ladder, and jump into a barge. And the sea is not very calm, at times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when you got to the bottom of the [LAUGHS] ladder, the barge drifted away, it was going backwards and forwards. So you have to jump into the-- be fortunate to get into the barge. And then we went in, onto the beaches-- and Arromanches. What's your first recollections of landing in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you do know how thankful you was until you landed on terra firma. Because when you're on the water, you get hit with something, you go down, there's no-- but on the ground, you're at least, you're safer. And when you saw a Royal Ensign in the distance, you thought, well, as least we're that far. We've gone that far. I saw the Royal Ensign, and I thought to myself, I wish I could hoist the Union Jack over the Reichstag. [LAUGHS] But that didn't come my way, it went to the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- on the beaches, was it a plane run, or was there and gunfire?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of gunfire, especially at night. The-- we used to have what was known as tracer bullets. Every third bullet, when you fired it, it was like, illuminating, you could see where it was going. But then it would stop, after a few days, it would stop. Because it gave away your positions to the Germans, where they're coming from. So that was stopped straightaway. But at night, it was like one huge firework display-- with blue flashes, green flashes, red flashes, all over the place. Like one big firework display.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make much progress inland, or were you still--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did. At first-- first we did. The first three days, we got to [? Bayeux. ?] There's a cathedral there, so we was there. And then some bright officer, would you please, will you put up all the signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we put the signs up. And we was on traffic duty. People-- yeah, if you're in a foreign country, you don't rely on the signs. You go by your maps. And we put the signs up, because you don't know, people can turn the signs around and lead you the wrong way. And lorries were going around and around in a circle. So one of the drivers said, how do you get out this place? What have they done? They signed the road to go into Bayeux, but they didn't sign it to going out. [LAUGHS] So everybody's going around in a circle. So we got rid of that. And then, the roads got very quiet. The traffic went to a trickle, and there wasn't much doing. So I stopped the driver, I said, where's all the traffic gone to? Are they going somewhere else? He said, don't you know? He says, that Mulberry Harbour, the wind had broken it, and there was a terrible gale out there, they can't land a thing. The medical supplies and food was being rationed. And ammunition was being rationed. He said, they can't-- they can't land a thing. They don't know what to do. Because we had just landed. If you're not getting supplies, then how long can you stay there? So Montgomery went down to the beaches. That's what I-- here, you had to have a proper field commander on the spot. It was no good directing the war from the War Office.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like in the-- when the war first started, if a German plane went over France, you had to find a War Office, if you can shoot it down. [LAUGHS] So he went down to the beaches. He surveyed the situation, he said, unload the ships. Whatever gets through, gets through. Some of them didn't-- the waves turned them over.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like on a day-to-day with the local population? Did you have any contact with people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't interfere with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the feeling that you were well-received?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had the feeling we were well-received. It didn't take long before the children come around. Say, have you got chocolate for mama, have you got soap for papas? We said no, but we've got sweets for your sister. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have much mixing? Was there any evenings after work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no, we was on duty 24 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How quickly did you progress through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we pushed through, we was going through France. Through Falaise there, where there was a lot of fighting. Till--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved very much with the fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with the actual fighting. With the roads. We was up at the front putting up the road signs. Because when a strange driver comes off from France-- from England, he's going to France. First of all, he's on the wrong side of the road. And then there's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] speed regulations. And then he doesn't know the roads, and you can't rely on the signs, so you have to go buy a map or ask someone where to go. Because a lot of them got detached, instead of turning right, they turned left, they got detached from the units. And we went on till-- we got to Amiens in September. That was the Jewish new year. We got there. So I got the-- the days off, the two days I got off. The timetables, duties were rearranged, so I got those two days off. While I was there, in Amiens, the message comes through, pack your things. The campaign in Orleans is broken and it's all gone wrong. You have to go out there, you've got to get them all out. It all went wrong. From there we had to go through, so we had to go through-- so right through France, right through Belgium, Brussels. We stopped at a place called Le Vigne for a few hours, and then we went into Holland. Through Eindhoven, and then up to, the bridge before Haarlem, there's a bridge, Nijmegen, that was-- at that time-- was the longest-span bridge in Europe-- Nijmegen. And there was a lot of fighting there. And we had to get them back. The troops, the paratroopers, they carried 48 hours of rations and ammunition. They were there for 17 days. And they only had 48 hours of food and ammunition. So you imagine-- why would we, could we-- we got out. And then the winter set in, so it all quieted down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to, you mentioned you had the two days off for New Year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many other Jewish people in the regiments you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they come from all surrounding districts, you see. If we want to know, if you wanted to go to a shul, you make it your business to find out. So you went to the army headquarters where you got someone to inquire at headquarters. The Jewish chaplain would know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us, there used to be a shul, in Amiens, but the Germans made it into a furniture storage. So we'll soon make it back into a shul. In-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans had got funny ways, at times. While I was in Amiens, I went walking around, and I come across a cemetery. And the cemetery's got some kind of fascination, I don't know why. And I went in. And there's a civilian walking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, do you speak English? He said, of course I speak English, I come from London. I said, so [LAUGHS] what are you doing here? He said, I am from the Imperial War Graves Commission, and I look after the cemetery. And the Germans never interfered with me at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you look carefully, one half is German graves from the First World War, and one half is British from the First World War. And I look after it. I cut the grass, I look after everything there. And they never interfered with me at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: This person had been there throughout?

speaker: interviewee
text: Throughout the whole war he was there, and never got interfered with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come across any other unusual people? Did you come across many Jewish people, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Couldn't find any. We're-- going through-- going through Brussels, being a capital city, so we didn't stop there. We went straight through. But when we was-- after the fighting in-- in Haarlem died down, we got pulled back from Nijmegen, and we were static. In a little place called Zon-- Z-O-N-- a little place. We looked after the bridges, the Bailey bridges. And word come around, there's two tickets-- I think it was two tickets to go to Brussels for Christmas. And nobody wanted to go because they are all friendly in the villages there. They had their girlfriends there, and they were-- so they were invited out for Christmas. So I said, all right, I'll go to Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Brussels. The first thing you do, you look for a shul. I had the folder, certain army welfare services. I've got the Paris one, but the Brussels ones are lost, I haven't got it. I've got the Paris one. And I looked up the-- and it gave you a map with places of worship were-- Church of England, Catholic, synagogues-- I thought, there's a shul here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was like the main shul of Brussels. So I went there. And the-- the doors were open, the lights were on, and I went in, and no one there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sort of condition was the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a big lectern, as you go in, with a visitors book, you should sign it. All the Americans signed it, but what they shouldn't have done, they put Private First Class and their regiment and all that. Which they shouldn't have done, because someone can see what regiment, and it's bad intelligence. You just put your name, rank and number.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in, all the lights were on. I looked around, I couldn't believe it. There wasn't a scratch on the paintwork. It was in a beautiful condition. So I called out, hello, hello, anyone there? Eventually, a little woman come out. I said, you speak English? No, she speaks Yiddish. I says, good. I said, who are you? She said she's the shamash' wife. So I said, where's the shamash? He's downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have a flat attached to the shul, and he's got TV. He's downstairs in the bed. If you like, go down to see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went down to see him. She said, apart from taking the silver from the shul, they didn't touch anything. There wasn't a scratch on the paintwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- it-- it-- but it was [? true. ?] They wanted to impress the Belgians that they're not such bad people as people make them out to be or not, I don't know. It was hard to-- and this was Christmas, 1944, when the Ardennes thing broke loose.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said she's been hiding in the forest there, with two little children. And she's just come back from two months. She's just come back two months. What should she do? Because it looked as though the Germans were going to split the armies in two. She said, what should she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, stay where you are. They won't get-- they won't get far. This is their last big push. We know that, we've been waiting for it. This is their last chance, you stay where they-- they won't get through. And the-- Then, opposite, there's a cinema. I was at the shul. At the far end, at the road, there was like the Palais de Justice. Like the old bailey. And on top was a huge dome, something like a monument, but a lot bigger. But the Germans took it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, why did they take it off? She says, nobody knows. For some reason, they took it off. Why, nobody knows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And opposite was a cinema, and they have the prices of the films. And on the top was a big sign, Jews and dogs not permitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was still up there. But it was in German-- Jews and dogs forbidden. So I went in, I called the manager. I said, you see that? Well, you take that off. You see, being a policeman, you've got a bit of authority. I said, you take that off. He says, he can't, he hasn't got-- and I got him by the collar. I said, now, look, you listen to me, if the German's would tell you to take it off, you'd say yes. You wouldn't be able to get it off quick enough. I said, if you don't take that off, I'll close your cinema. So he says, he hasn't got this, and I says, well paste it over with a piece of paper. But get it off. The front tag was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there generally a lot of anti-Semitism around? Do you have a feeling that the people supported, to a certain extent, anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, in the occupied countries? No-- no, I didn't, in fact. Because those that collaborated with the Germans, once they were liberated, the people that lived in the villages used to take them out into the square, cut their hair off, and do all sorts of things. And we never interfered, it was nothing to do with us. The difference was the Germans used to employ local labor to do all sorts of jobs. The British, never. They'd done everything themselves. We had our own generators, our own water, everything self-efficient. We never relied on civilians to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're moving forward. Your next stage was-- where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed that winter in-- just outside the Eindhoven, we got pulled back from Njimenen again. And this little place, Zon. And-- while I was on the bridge-- I wasn't on the bridge, I was in the office at the time-- someone come in, a British driver. He was all shaken up.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said he's had an accident on the bridge. He's knocked a boy down. He's badly hurt, what should he do? I said, just get on your lorry and go. There's so many thousands of troops got killed in Orleans, so what civilian boy is going to make all the difference? Nobody-- civilians don't come into it. But we had instructions not to get mixed up with civilian people. So we went in. I said, look, someone has come in, there's been a nasty accident. Can we take a lorry and take him down to the hospital? They said, no, you mustn't get mixed up with the civilians. Which country was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland. Just past-- north of Eindhoven. I said, we can't leave him on the road. In the snow. It was snow, it was in December. Are we just going to leave him there like that? He said, look, the best thing to do, take him, put him in the lorry. Take him up to the hospital, in Eindhoven, which was about 10 miles, but don't go in. Take him up to the hospital and let them take over. They were very nice people, very hospitable. We used to go in there every evening. In the cafe, they had the cafe. We would just go and-- even if you didn't buy anything, they had a big fire there and it was bitterly cold, so we used to go into the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how can you go in and tell these people that their only son, he was 17, he got knocked down by this lorry? Someone went in and said, all we can do is take him down to the hospital in the lorry. So they said, oh, no, you have to wait for the priest. You've got to wait for the priest. I said, he's badly hurt. Oh, no, it's God's wish this should happen, and you've got to wait for the priest.

speaker: interviewee
text: But to go and get a priest, in the snow, in the slush. Till he comes with his robes, on a bicycle, through the fields. It took about 20 minutes-- a half hour. And he administered the last rites. Then she said, now you can take him to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time we got there, we said to the doctor, what's his chances of survival? He said, if you had brought him a half-hour earlier, he'd have had a 50/50 chance. But as it stands, he'll live about another 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too late. But that was their belief, that it's God's wish this should happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- that was after Christmas. Nothing happened in January.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew the big push is coming in March somewhere. Around about March, I got called into the office. You've got a holiday coming up, prunes or something? Prunes, no. I said, You don't mean Purim, do you? [LAUGHS] Well, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, there's a service for the Jewish personnel in Eindhoven. If you want to go, I'll order the roads. I said, of course I'll go. So I went there. That was the first time I come across Captain Hartman, It was Captain Hartman And then the senior Jewish chaplain, Isaac Levy, and the squadron [INAUDIBLE] the squadron leader, Sanker-- he was for the RAF. And that was the first time I saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people? Who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: One was-- huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was senior Jewish chaplain, Isaac Levy, one was the RAF chaplain, squadron leader Sanker, and Captain Hartman, he was second army chaplain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had a little service, and they take-- take everybody's name, just in case you get mentioned in the paper somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There must have been about 25-- 20, 25. One of the fellows has an all argument with Isaac Levy, because he married took place in the Bayswater shul, and the marriage didn't work out, so that was his fault. He said, all right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, about two weeks later, I got a letter from home. Who is this Captain Hartman? He's going to send a letter to my sister at the time, that he saw me, I'm all right. And got well and everything. I'll come home all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always kept that letter. And that's on exhibition at the Sternberg Center in London. She said, they caught such a fright. Because if you get a letter from an officer, there's only one thought that comes to your mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had such a fright when the saw the-- from the Jewish chaplain, it could only mean one thing. Till they opened it and they read it. So he was the only one in the six years of war, that ever took the trouble to send home a letter and say that they'd seen me. I'd seen other parties-- Did you know any of the other 25 people who were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were from all parts of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Or from the RAF, [INAUDIBLE] all different branches of the services. Nobody wanted to mix with the military police, anyway, so it didn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many other Jewish people who didn't attend, who weren't interested?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't see any. We didn't come across any, . And after that, I went home for Passover. That was April, and then the big push into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just as I come back, that's when the-- Belsen thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go back to? Where was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the time I left to the time I come back, they pushed on. I went back into Germany somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- Gottingen. I think a place called Gottingen. Where they used to make steel. That-- And from there, we were in the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we just take a break here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all right.
